analysis: SM70 introduced tensor cores: 8x8x4 matrix multiply per warp per cycle
SIMT GEMM: 32 scalar FMAs per warp vs tensor core: 512 FMAs per warp
Tensor cores require specific data layout and WMMA intrinsics
Must restructure algorithm around 8x8x4 MMA primitive
schedule_reasoning: Volta WMMA: warp-level 8x8x4 MMA instruction
64x64 warp tile = (64/8)×(64/8) = 64 WMMA ops per warp
2 pipeline stages: Volta lacks cp.async, uses explicit shared memory staging
96KB shared memory vs 48KB on Pascal - enables larger tiles
This is the paradigm shift: warp-cooperative matrix ops vs thread-scalar
concept: Volta tensor cores - SIMT to WMMA transition

constraints.special: First INT8 tensor cores for quantized inference

analysis: Turing added INT8 tensor cores: 16x8x32 MMA for int8→int32
2x throughput vs f16 (8 bits vs 16 bits per element)
Requires pre-quantized weights and dynamic activation quantization
INT8 MMA accumulates to int32 to avoid overflow
schedule_reasoning: 16x8x32 INT8 MMA: 4096 int8 ops per warp per cycle
K=64 tile: 64 int8 elements = 64 bytes, fits 16-element alignment
int32 accumulator: 32K int8 products before overflow risk
Saturate mode: clamp to [-128, 127] on int8 output
Turing brought quantized inference to GPUs - enabled INT8 ResNet, BERT
concept: Turing INT8 tensor cores - quantized inference

analysis: Stride=2 means input positions 0,2,4,6... are used, 1,3,5,7... skipped
Standard implicit GEMM assumes contiguous input positions
Memory coalescing broken: threads access positions 0,2,4... not 0,1,2...
StrideSupport::kStrided iterator handles non-unit stride correctly
schedule_reasoning: kStrided iterator multiplies spatial indices by stride factor
Offset table precomputes strided positions: 0, 2*C, 4*C, 6*C...
Loads remain vectorized: load 8 elements at position h*stride, w*stride
Output spatial dims: H_out = (H + 2*pad - R) / stride + 1
Common in ResNet: first conv 7x7 stride 2, pooling stride 2
concept: Strided convolution - non-unit stride implicit GEMM

constraints.special: Dilation=2 - effective 5x5 receptive field with 3x3 filter

analysis: Dilation spreads filter taps: 3x3 dilation=2 → effective 5x5 receptive field
Memory access pattern: h, h+2, h+4 - skips intermediate rows
Cache line loads 128 bytes but uses only positions 0,2,4 - 50% waste
Common in semantic segmentation (DeepLab) for multi-scale context
schedule_reasoning: kStrided iterator supports both stride and dilation parameters
Dilation factor applied to filter spatial offsets: r*dilation, s*dilation
Same tile shape as unit dilation - dilation only affects iterator math
Output size unchanged: dilation expands receptive field, not output
Useful for dense prediction: segmentation, depth estimation

constraints.special: Groups=32 - each group processes C/32=8 input channels

analysis: G=32 groups, C/G=8 channels per group - very small K dimension
Each group is 32×56×56×8 → 32×56×56×8 - tiny GEMM
32 separate kernels have massive launch overhead
Grouped conv should batch all groups into single kernel
schedule_reasoning: Batched grouped conv: all G groups in single kernel launch
Grid dimension includes group index: (tiles_h, tiles_w, N*G)
Each threadblock processes one group's spatial tile
Filter tensor shape: [K, R, S, C/G] with G copies
Used in: ResNeXt (G=32), ShuffleNet, MobileNet (G=C for depthwise)

constraints.special: Depthwise: groups=C, one filter per channel, K=C

analysis: Depthwise: each filter operates on single input channel
K dimension = R*S = 9 (3x3 filter) - tensor cores need K≥16
Implicit GEMM overhead dominates for such small K
Better to use direct convolution for depthwise
schedule_reasoning: Direct convolution: explicit 3x3 loop, no im2col overhead
No tensor cores: K=9 cannot fill MMA instruction
Each thread computes multiple output positions
Filter (3x3=9 elements) fits in registers
Depthwise is memory-bound anyway - compute efficiency less critical
concept: Depthwise convolution - direct algorithm for small K
